content.text: For a
Claude + GPT multi-model app
, the best routing design is usually an
“Amigo pattern” interpreted as friendly specialist collaboration

: one entry point, a lightweight router, and clearly separated specialist lanes for Claude-strength tasks, GPT-strength tasks, and fallback/escalation behavior. In production, the most reliable strategy is not “pick the best model globally,” but “route by task class, risk, latency budget, context size, and cost ceiling,” because both Anthropic and OpenAI guidance point toward task-specific workflows, routing, and tuning rather than one-model-for-everything.

attrs.alt: Claude + GPT routing strategies (Amigo pattern) concept diagram

content.text: What the pattern means
There does not appear to be one authoritative public standard named the
“Amigo pattern”
for Claude+GPT routing, but the closest useful interpretation from the available sources is a
specialist collaboration pattern

: a router decides which expert lane should handle the request, and downstream specialists operate with their own prompts, tools, and budgets. That maps directly onto Anthropic’s routing workflow guidance, which defines routing as classifying an input and directing it to a specialized follow-up task. So architecturally, the “Amigo” idea works as a human-friendly name for a
router plus specialists
system.
Core routing principle

Anthropic explicitly recommends routing when there are distinct categories better handled separately, and gives the example of sending easy questions to smaller cheaper models and harder unusual questions to more capable models. OpenAI’s deployment checklist similarly recommends using lower reasoning effort for extraction, routing, classification, and simple rewrites, and higher effort for diagnosis, planning, and reasoning-heavy code or tradeoff tasks. Together, these support a practical rule:
route first by workload shape, then tune model depth second
Why multi-model at all

A multi-model architecture is usually justified by four production concerns: capability fit, cost control, latency control, and resilience. Several routing guides in the search results emphasize that different requests need different quality levels, and that every production workflow should have both a primary and a fallback model rather than betting everything on one provider. This matters especially for user-facing apps where outages, rate limits, or latency spikes must not take down the entire experience.
Best default architecture
A strong baseline architecture is:
Entry API or gateway
receives the request.
Router
classifies task type and operating constraints.
Claude lane
handles Claude-favored work.
GPT lane
handles GPT-favored work.
Fallback lane
retries or degrades gracefully across vendors.
Unified telemetry layer
logs chosen model, latency, token cost, outcome quality, and fallback reason.
This is the simplest version that still gives real production benefits.
Router design

The router itself should be as cheap and deterministic as possible. Anthropic’s routing pattern shows a two-step architecture where the system first classifies the request, then sends the original input to the right specialist. OpenAI’s guidance further suggests that routing and classification are low-effort tasks, which means the router does not need the most expensive reasoning path.
Don’t overcomplicate the router

The easiest mistake is building a very smart router before you have evidence you need one. Anthropic’s broader agent guidance repeatedly says to start with the simplest solution and add complexity only when it demonstrably improves outcomes. In practice, that means starting with a rule-based routing table plus a light classifier before moving to learned or multi-stage routing.
Good routing dimensions
The most useful routing inputs, consistently reflected across the sources, are:
Task class
: coding, extraction, research, structured output, support reply, planning.
Complexity
: simple vs multi-step ambiguous reasoning.
Latency budget
: interactive chat vs background workflow.
Context length
: short request vs large context job.
Cost ceiling
: premium allowed or budget mode.
Risk level
: high-stakes action vs low-stakes draft.
These features are more reliable than vague “which model is smarter?” heuristics.
Claude lane strategy

From the available sources, Claude is especially well-positioned in routing systems where you want a specialized lane for complex coding, multi-step workflows, or well-scoped specialist handling. Anthropic’s own routing guidance explicitly frames routing as a way to send hard cases to more capable models and easy cases to cheaper ones. So in a Claude+GPT stack, Claude often becomes the lane for
deep synthesis, coding, longer-context analysis, or specialist prompts tuned carefully for a known domain
GPT lane strategy

OpenAI’s deployment checklist shows strong controls for adjustable reasoning effort, verbosity, tool search, built-in tools, background processing, prompt cache keys, and long-running workflow continuation. That makes GPT a strong candidate for workloads where you want fine control over reasoning budget, compact or verbose outputs, built-in tool ecosystems, or long-running stateful workflows through the Responses API. In a multi-model design, GPT often becomes the lane for
tool-heavy operational workflows, structured continuation, or products already standardized on OpenAI-native runtime features
Don’t route by brand alone

A common mistake is creating rules like “all coding goes to Claude” or “all JSON goes to GPT” forever. Sources on multi-model routing emphasize that production routing should be based on evaluated workload categories and quality thresholds, not static brand mythology. The router should be adjustable as your evals show where one provider is stronger for your exact tasks.
The strongest first version
The strongest first implementation is usually a
manual task map

with explicit slots, not automatic universal intelligence. One routing guide recommends defining named slots tied to roles such as “fast-chat,” “deep-reasoning,” “structured-output,” and “fallback,” then mapping models to those slots rather than hardcoding one model everywhere in application logic. This is a very good pattern because it lets you swap providers later without rewriting the app.
Suggested slot design
A practical Claude+GPT slot system looks like this:
router
: cheapest reliable classifier or deterministic rule engine.
fast-chat
: low-latency general interactive responses.
deep-reasoning
: complex analysis, plans, debugging, architectural tradeoffs.
structured-output
: extraction, schemas, classification, machine-consumed JSON.
tool-heavy-agent
: workflows with many tools or long-running state.
fallback
: second-vendor recovery path.
Then you attach Claude or GPT variants to each slot based on your eval results.
Fallback chains matter

Every production workflow should have a fallback, preferably across providers. One guide recommends a primary provider plus fallback providers from different infrastructures, because rate limits, 5xx errors, policy mismatches, and latency spikes are all normal operational realities. This is one of the best reasons to adopt Claude+GPT together: multi-vendor resilience is better than single-vendor optimism.
Fallback should be workflow-aware

Not all fallbacks should be identical. A high-risk financial workflow may require strict equivalence and perhaps escalation to a human if the primary model fails, while a low-risk summarization flow can transparently retry with another provider or even degrade to a cheaper model. So fallback chains should be defined per workflow, not globally.
Cost routing

Cost-based routing has very high leverage and is one of the simplest strategies to implement. Several sources recommend reserving premium reasoning models for truly complex tasks, while using cheaper models for routing, extraction, classification, and simple transformations. This aligns well with both Anthropic’s example of small-model vs capable-model routing and OpenAI’s recommendation to keep low-effort reasoning for lighter tasks.
Latency routing

Latency-aware routing is also important in full-stack apps. User-facing chat often needs fast response start times, while asynchronous jobs can tolerate slower high-capability models or background execution paths. So a good architecture keeps separate routing profiles for
interactive
versus
background
workloads.
Context-aware routing

Context size is one of the most practical routing inputs. Multi-model guides in the results specifically call out context-window requirements as a routing dimension, since some models are better suited to larger prompts or long historical state than others. A router should therefore inspect approximate token size before model selection, not after the request fails or becomes too expensive.
Risk-aware routing

Risk is often under-modeled in first-generation routers. Compliance-sensitive calls, customer-visible high-stakes answers, or actions that mutate systems should route differently from low-risk drafts or internal brainstorms. In practice, this usually means stricter prompts, more capable models, more evaluation, and safer fallback behavior for high-risk lanes.
Evaluation loop

A multi-model router is only as good as its evaluation loop. Anthropic’s effective-agents guidance stresses measurement and iterative improvement, and several routing sources emphasize quality floors, escalation rules, and logging model choices, cost, latency, and outcome quality. So routing should be treated as an empirically tuned system, not a one-time architecture diagram.
Logging requirements
Your gateway or orchestration layer should log at least:
Incoming task type and route decision.
Chosen model and provider.
Prompt or policy version.
Token usage and estimated cost.
Latency and streaming start latency.
Fallback or retry events.
Eval score or human override outcome.
Without this, you cannot improve routing rationally.
Tool and runtime alignment

Routing is not only about model skill; it is also about runtime and tool compatibility. OpenAI’s Responses API supports built-in tools, background jobs, tool search, compaction, WebSocket mode, and encrypted reasoning continuation, which may make GPT the better lane for certain operational workflows. Anthropic’s guidance, by contrast, strongly emphasizes simple composable workflows, routing, orchestrator-workers, and well-designed tool interfaces, which may favor Claude in flows where specialized prompt design and transparent tool use matter most.
Good “Amigo” operating model
If you want a memorable operational version of the “Amigo pattern,” use three logical friends:
Router amigo
: decides where the work goes.
Claude amigo
: handles Claude-optimized specialist work.
GPT amigo
: handles GPT-optimized specialist work.
A fourth hidden friend is the
fallback/evaluator layer
, which checks quality and reroutes or escalates when needed. That mental model is simple enough for teams to reason about without collapsing into chaos.
Best first production rollout
A solid first rollout looks like this:
Define 3 to 5 workload classes.
Create one routing table mapping each class to a primary and fallback model.
Start with manual rules plus a lightweight classifier only where needed.
Log all decisions and outcomes.
Add quality thresholds and escalation after you collect real traffic data.
This is much safer than launching with opaque “auto” routing everywhere.
Interview-ready explanation

A strong answer is: “In a Claude+GPT multi-model app, I use a router-first specialist architecture. The router classifies requests by task class, complexity, latency budget, context length, cost ceiling, and risk level, then sends them to a specialist lane rather than one universal model. Claude and GPT each get assigned to workload slots where they perform best in our evals, and every workflow has a cross-provider fallback. I keep the router cheap and simple, log every routing decision, and continuously tune the table using cost, latency, and quality data. The core idea is the Anthropic routing pattern applied across vendors, with OpenAI runtime features informing which lane handles tool-heavy or reasoning-heavy work.”
Best Practices
When working with claude + gpt routing strategies (amigo pattern), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
Common Mistakes to Avoid
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with claude + gpt routing strategies (amigo pattern). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
Performance and Scalability
As your usage of claude + gpt routing strategies (amigo pattern) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
